title: Spanish Tortilla Española

ingredients.name: potatoes, peeled and sliced 3 mm thin

subs.name: Sweet potato can replace up to half the potato for a slightly sweet variation, though the texture will be softer
Spring onion (hara pyaz) or shallots work well; some Spanish cooks omit the onion entirely for a purer potato flavour
Any neutral refined oil; ghee gives a richer flavour in the potato confiting step

steps.text: Peel the potatoes and slice them into thin rounds about 3 mm thick — a mandoline or careful knife work both work. Slice the onion into thin half-moons. Season both with a generous pinch of salt.

Heat the olive oil in a 20–22 cm frying pan or tawa over medium-low flame. Add the potato and onion slices, gently pressing them down. Cook slowly for 18–20 minutes, turning occasionally with a spatula, until the potatoes are completely tender and pale gold rather than crisp — you are confiting them in oil, not frying.

While the potatoes cook, crack the eggs into a large bowl, season with salt, and beat well with a fork. When the potatoes are ready, lift them out with a slotted spoon and add them to the beaten egg, pressing gently to submerge. Let the mixture rest for 5 minutes so the egg starts to soak into the potato.

Pour away most of the oil from the pan, leaving just a thin coating. Reheat the pan over medium flame, then pour in the potato-egg mixture and spread it evenly. Cook for 4–5 minutes until the edges are set and the centre still looks slightly wobbly when you shake the pan.

Place a large flat plate or tawa lid over the pan. In one confident motion, flip the pan so the tortilla lands on the plate cooked-side up. Slide it back into the pan raw-side down and cook for another 2–3 minutes until just set through — it should be slightly creamy in the centre rather than fully firm. Slide onto a plate and serve warm or at room temperature.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: olive-oil → Any neutral refined oil; ghee gives a richer flavour in the potato confiting step; potato → Sweet potato can replace up to half the potato for a slightly sweet variation, though the texture will be softer; onion → Spring onion (hara pyaz) or shallots work well; some Spanish cooks omit the onion entirely for a purer potato flavour
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A non-stick tawa is ideal for this recipe and makes the flip much less stressful than a stainless steel pan. Regular potatoes from any Indian market work perfectly — no special variety needed. Any neutral oil (sunflower, groundnut) replaces olive oil without noticeable loss of flavour. The flip step is the only tricky part: practise with a flat plate slightly larger than the tawa to build confidence. Adding a pinch of black pepper or a green chilli to the egg mix is a popular Indian adaptation.
